Title: Isolating an active and inactive CACTA transposon from lettuce color mutants and characterizing their family

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Dietary antioxidant important for human nutrition and health
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Insertion of active CACTA transposon LsC1 into the ANS 5' UTR strongly represses ANS expression, greatly lowers leaf cyanidin/anthocyanin accumulation, and causes light green leaves with red sectors due to somatic excision.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LsC1 is a 3,913-nt nonautonomous CACTA transposon inserted between the 28th and 29th nucleotides upstream of the ANS start codon in GSL. 2. Intact terminal inverted repeats enable somatic excision, restoring local ANS expression and producing red sectors on otherwise green leaves. 3. Homozygous LsC1/LsC1 plants showed nearly eliminated ANS expression and the lowest cyanidin among mutant genotypes.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cool fluorescent light growth conditions promoting flavonoid accumulation
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: CACTA (En/Spm) superfamily nonautonomous DNA transposon with 28-nt imperfect TIRs
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Putative transposase complex components TnpD; TnpA
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Candidate-gene sequencing; Sanger sequencing; PCR genotyping; RNA-seq; RT-qPCR; UPLC-MS/MS metabolite profiling; segregation analysis
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful as a naturally occurring allele affecting anthocyanin level and leaf color; may help develop green lettuce with elevated total flavonoids, though instability from excision is a caution.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Trait instability due to active somatic excision causes sectoring and phenotypic mosaicism.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: LsC1 is the causal insertional element but is not itself a metabolism enzyme or TF; its effect is mediated by ANS repression.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Dietary antioxidant important for human nutrition and health
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Truncated inactive derivative LsC2 in the ANS 5' UTR allows low but detectable ANS expression and intermediate cyanidin accumulation, causing stable dark green leaves without red sectors.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LsC2 is a 1,096-nt truncated derivative of LsC1 inserted at the same ANS 5' UTR site. 2. Loss of one TIR renders LsC2 inactive, explaining the stable non-sectored phenotype. 3. LsC2/LsC2 plants had higher ANS expression and cyanidin than LsC1/LsC1 plants but far less than wild type.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cool fluorescent light growth conditions promoting flavonoid accumulation
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Truncated CACTA-family nonautonomous DNA transposon lacking one TIR
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Candidate-gene sequencing; Sanger sequencing; PCR genotyping; RT-qPCR; UPLC-MS/MS metabolite profiling; segregation analysis
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Stable allele for reducing anthocyanin while maintaining relatively high total flavonoid content in green lettuce.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced cyanidin relative to wild type red lettuce.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: LsC2 likely formed by incomplete excision of LsC1 and is not mobile.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Anthocyanidin-forming branch downstream of dihydroflavonol reductase
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts colorless leucoanthocyanidins to red anthocyanidins (also described as leucoanthocyanidin dioxygenase activity).
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms the anthocyanidin product leading to cyanidin accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key control point for cyanidin accumulation in this study, though not explicitly claimed as rate-limiting enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Anthocyanin pigment and dietary antioxidant important for human nutrition and health
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher ANS expression strongly promotes leaf cyanidin accumulation and red coloration; transposon-mediated repression of ANS lowers cyanidin and shifts leaves to green.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. RNA-seq identified ANS as the only flavonoid gene with an expression pattern matching the color phenotypes (NAR > GSL-DG > GSL). 2. LsC1 and LsC2 insertions in the ANS 5' UTR strongly alter ANS expression, with wild type highest, LsC2 intermediate, and LsC1 lowest. 3. ANS expression showed a strong positive correlation with cyanidin concentration across the four isogenic genotypes. 4. Reduced ANS expression was associated with increased quercetin, while kaempferol was unaffected.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in leaves; expression highest in red NAR, intermediate in GSL-DG and GSL-DG-(r), lowest in GSL under cool fluorescent light
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cool fluorescent light promotes flavonoid accumulation and was used for expression/metabolite comparisons
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq differential expression; Sanger sequencing of ANS and UTRs; PCR genotyping of insertion alleles; RT-qPCR; UPLC-MS/MS flavonoid quantification; correlation analysis across isogenic genotypes
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating ANS expression/alleles can tune lettuce anthocyanin and quercetin composition; mutant alleles may support nutritionally enhanced green romaine-type lines.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced cyanidin/red pigmentation when ANS is repressed; possible flux redistribution to quercetin.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ANS is single copy in lettuce. The cv Salinas reference allele contains an early stop codon and is described as an ANS nonsense mutant; regulatory context likely explains why Salinas still differs metabolically from GSL.
